title: Belief Over History: Smoothing vs. Filtering

runtime.expected_outcome: q(s_tau | o_{1:T}) for past tau gets sharper after future observations (smoothing effect) vs. filter-only beliefs.

math.latex: q(s_\tau \mid o_{1:T}) \propto q(s_\tau \mid o_{1:\tau}) \sum_{s_{\tau+1}} q(s_{\tau+1} \mid o_{\tau+1:T}) \, P(s_{\tau+1} \mid s_\tau, a_\tau)

symbols.smoothed belief: q(s_tau | o_{1:T}) using all observations, past and future

audiences.kid: Sometimes a clue later helps you understand a clue you saw earlier. Looking back with new info makes the past clearer.
audiences.real: The sweep_state_beliefs action in the POMDP bundle is a forward-backward smoother, not just a filter. Future observations retrospectively sharpen past beliefs.
audiences.equation: q(s_tau | o_{1:T}) combines the forward filtered belief with the backward message. Minimising F at every tau is equivalent.
audiences.derivation: The belief chain q(s_{1:T} | o_{1:T}) factorises under the mean-field assumption. Each marginal q(s_tau | o_{1:T}) is updated by the product of forward message alpha_tau and backward message beta_tau (Rauch-Tung-Striebel in the Gaussian case).

orchestrate.objective: Show that the sweep_state_beliefs action produces smoothed, not just filtered, posteriors.
orchestrate.role: Smoothing-vs-filtering tutor using the bundle's forward-backward sweep.
orchestrate.context: corridor_turns 5x5, run the agent to the goal and compare q(s_1 | o_1) vs. q(s_1 | o_{1:T}).

credits: Parr, Pezzulo & Friston (2022), Active Inference, MIT Press (CC BY-NC-ND), Ch 4 & App B.
Rauch, Tung & Striebel (1965), Maximum likelihood estimates of linear dynamic systems.
